ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for E-fu noodles or black truffle braised noodles. FOODON_03315952 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles) is the closest defensible parent category. No specific code exists for E-fu noodles or black truffle braised noodle preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for E-fu noodles or black truffle braised noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of black truffle braised E-fu noodles is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a single plate at a dim sum or Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked egg noodles / braised noodle dish — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Egg noodles, cooked, enriched, with added salt, NDB 20113) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for braised noodle preparations, adjusted for black truffle and sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists for black truffle braised E-fu noodles in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked egg noodles (NDB 20113) as the dominant component (~65% by weight), with adjustments for braising sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, chicken broth) and black truffle (~3–5% by weight). Black truffle contributes negligible macronutrients at typical usage levels. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce. Fat is moderately elevated due to cooking oil and sesame oil. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and wheat-based noodle products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (GI ~40, boiled) and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black truffle braised E-fu noodles. E-fu noodles are deep-fried then dried egg-wheat noodles that are rehydrated and braised. The closest approved GI source entry is boiled egg noodles (GI ~40, low) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Braising in a savoury sauce does not substantially alter GI relative to boiling. GI of 40 is used as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (40 / 100) × 26.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving = 31.8 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined wheat-egg noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 40 (low) but a glycemic load of ~31.8 per standard 300 g serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from braising sauce and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) and moderate fat (~4.5 g per 100 g) from cooking oil and sesame oil are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Truffle Braised E-fu Noodles is a premium Cantonese noodle dish featuring E-fu noodles (also known as yi mein or yee fu mein) — a distinctive type of pre-fried, dried egg-and-wheat noodle with a golden colour and characteristically soft, silky, slightly chewy texture after braising. The noodles are braised in a rich, aromatic sauce incorporating black truffle, chicken broth, oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger, which imparts deep umami flavour and a luxurious truffle aroma. This dish is commonly served at Cantonese dim sum restaurants and Chinese banquet settings in Singapore and Hong Kong, often as a premium menu item. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 504 kcal, 17.4 g protein, 13.5 g fat, and 79.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high (~1260 mg per serving) due to the savoury braising sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate to high (~31.8), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Black truffle contributes negligible macronutrients at typical restaurant usage levels but adds significant flavour complexity and premium positioning. The dish contains wheat gluten, egg, soy, and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in truffle noodles?
Is E-fu noodles high in sodium?
What is the GI of E-fu noodles?
What allergens are in E-fu noodles?
What is the difference between E-fu noodles and regular egg noodles?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for black truffle braised E-fu noodles exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked egg noodle data combined with adjustments for braising sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled egg noodles. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation basis.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 20113, egg noodles cooked) as the dominant component, adjusted for braising sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, chicken broth) and black truffle. Black truffle is used at approximately 3–5% by weight in restaurant preparations and contributes negligible macronutrients. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce in the braising sauce. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in this preparation). Vitamin A is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the source database. Vitamin B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, and selenium are -1 as these are not reported in the estimation source for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled egg noodles (GI ~40) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Halal status is marked false as the dish is served at a dim sum restaurant where pork-based ingredients may be present in the braising stock; halal certification would need to be confirmed per restaurant. The dish contains wheat gluten, egg, soy, and sesame allergens.
